---
title: "Module_01_WKSP_04"
author: "Justin Proctor"
date: "2026-05-28"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## MB5370 Module 1: Programming Fundamentals
## Workshop 4: The Developer's Toolbox and AI

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r}
#install.packages("ellmer")
#install.packages("chattr")
```

```{r}
#install.packages("palmerpenguins")
```

```{r using github copilot to pull penguins package and load it}
#load tidyverse and palmerpenguins libraries


library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)

# Create a ggplot scatter plot using the penguins dataset. 
# Plot bill_length_mm on the x-axis and bill_depth_mm on the y-axis.
# Color points by species and use geom_point with size 3.
# Add a minimal theme and clean labels.
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Penguin Bill Dimensions",
       x = "Bill Length (mm)",
       y = "Bill Depth (mm)",
       color = "Species")

```

```{r open-ended comparative plotting using the penguins dataset}
#please create a boxplot comparing flipper_length_mm across different species in the palmerpenguins dataset. 
#place flipper length on the y axis and species on the x axis.
#facet these data by island and add a minimal theme with clean labels.
#please also use a custom color palette of your choice to differentiate the species in the boxplot.
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm, fill = species)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~ island) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Flipper Length by Species and Island",
       x = "Species",
       y = "Flipper Length (mm)",
       fill = "Species") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Adelie" = "#1f77b4", "Chinstrap" = "#ff7f0e", "Gentoo" = "#2ca02c"))
```



```{r testing chattr (chatgpt) plug in by asking it to produce the following code}
library(ggplot2)

data(iris)

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) +
  geom_point()

```

```{r conditional logic - filtering data using base R}
# Simple Example: Checking Sea Surface Temperature (SST)
sst <- 30.2

if (sst > 29.5) {
  print("Warning: Marine heatwave threshold exceeded!")
} else {
  print("SST remains within baseline parameters.")
}
```

```{r conditional logic - filtering data using Tidyverse Alternative}
# Load tidyverse (if not already loaded)
library(tidyverse)

# Sample coral data
coral_monitoring <- tibble(
  site = c("Site_A", "Site_B", "Site_C"),
  depth_m = c(12, 35, 8)
)

# Classify depth using if_else
coral_monitoring <- coral_monitoring %>% 
  mutate(zone = if_else(depth_m > 30, "Deep Reef", "Shallow Reef"))

```

```{r conditional logic - filtering data using case_when()}
coral_monitoring <- coral_monitoring %>% 
  mutate(reef_category = case_when(
    depth_m < 10  ~ "Lagoon / Flats",
    depth_m <= 30 ~ "Crest / Slope",
    depth_m > 30  ~ "Mesophotic / Deep",
    TRUE          ~ "Unclassified" # Catch-all remainder
  ))
```

```{r automation with base R}
# Classic loop anatomy
for (year in 2020:2024) {
  print(paste("Processing climate data for year:", year))
}
```

```{r}
# Loop across index sequences
transect_lengths <- c(50, 100, 25, 75)

for (i in seq_along(transect_lengths)) {
  print(paste("Transect number", i, "measures", transect_lengths[i], "metres."))
}

```

```{r automation with tidyverse alternative - standard for-loop}
site_areas <- c(144, 400, 625)
results <- numeric(length(site_areas)) # Must build an empty container first

for (i in seq_along(site_areas)) {
  results[i] <- sqrt(site_areas[i])
}

results
```

```{r automation with tidyverse alternative - purrr}
library(purrr)
# Map directly and define your expected output type explicitly
mapped_results <- map_dbl(site_areas, sqrt)

```

```{r automation with tidyverse alternative - Iterating Across Complex Groups}
# Iterate a summary function over split lists of data
iris %>% 
  split(.$Species) %>% 
  map(~summary(.x))

```

```{r chatr task using purrr}
# List of vectors with fish counts
fish_counts <- list(
  lake1 = c(10, 12, 13, 9),
  lake2 = c(8, 6, 7),
  lake3 = c(14, 15, 13, 16, 15)
)

# For-loop to calculate the mean of each vector
fish_means <- numeric(length(fish_counts))
for (i in seq_along(fish_counts)) {
  fish_means[i] <- mean(fish_counts[[i]])
}
fish_means
```

```{r Function Construction Blueprint}
# Function Definition
calculate_coral_mortality <- function(initial_count, surviving_count) {
  
  # Logic safety switch using our conditional tools!
  if (initial_count <= 0) {
    stop("Initial count must be greater than zero.")
  }
  
  mortality_rate <- (initial_count - surviving_count) / initial_count
  return(mortality_rate)
}

# Utilizing your custom function
calculate_coral_mortality(initial_count = 120, surviving_count = 84)

```

```{r Exercise 4: Custom Function Construction Task}
convert_temp_c_to_f <- function(celsius) {
  if (celsius < -273.15) {
    stop("Temperature cannot be below absolute zero (-273.15°C).")
  }
 
  fahrenheit <- (celsius * 9/5) + 32
  return(fahrenheit)
}

# Valid test
convert_temp_c_to_f(25)
```
